Title: Enrichment of tomato fruit with health-promoting anthocyanins by expression of select transcription factors

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: MicroTom; phenotype transferred to Money Maker, Ailsa Craig, VF36
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Use coordinated fruit-specific activation of anthocyanin regulatory modules to enrich tomato fruit with anthocyanins in both peel and flesh. Best deployment is fruit/ripening-specific expression to avoid ectopic pigmentation and vegetative penalties. Can be combined with endogenous pathway optimization and transport/modification genes for stable, high anthocyanin accumulation. Monitor consumer traits, regulatory acceptance, and background-dependent expression.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression of select transcription factors caused massive accumulation of anthocyanins in tomato fruit, giving intense purple coloration and a threefold increase in hydrophilic antioxidant capacity; tomato-supplemented diet extended lifespan in cancer-susceptible mice.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression in tomato; HPLC/metabolite profiling; spectrophotometric anthocyanin quantification; antioxidant capacity assay (TEAC); gene-expression analysis by northern blot, subtractive hybridization and microarray; mouse dietary assay.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit ripening stage strongly affected phenotype because the E8 promoter activated pigmentation from mature green/breaker onward; anthocyanin accumulation discussed relative to light dependence in prior tomato systems.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Anthocyanin-enriched phenotype and transgene action were confined mainly to ripening fruit, with pigmentation beginning at the end of mature green stage and intensifying through breaker/red stages; no abnormal accumulation in stems or leaves.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not applicable for this generalized metabolic trait entry.
Common_Genes.Gene_Name: Anthocyanin metabolic engineering module (Del/Ros1-driven phenylpropanoid/flavonoid activation)
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Del; Ros1; PAL; CHI; F3H; F3'5'H; DFR; ANS; 3-GT; RT; 5-GT; anthocyanin acyltransferase; GST; PAT
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Suppression subtractive hybridization; northern blot; tomato PROFOOD oligonucleotide microarray; ArrayExpress A-MEXP-1417 and E-MEXP-1830; HPLC/ESI-MS metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The achieved anthocyanin levels were among the highest reported in tomato and comparable to berry anthocyanin concentrations. Major products were acylated delphinidin/petunidin 3-rutinoside-5-glucosides. Increased hydrophilic antioxidant capacity occurred without loss of lipophilic antioxidants.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious vegetative growth defects were reported in primary transformants. Possible tradeoffs not deeply assessed include altered phenylpropanoid partitioning, fruit quality changes, and regulatory issues around transgenes.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Coordinated regulation of phenylpropanoid/flavonoid metabolism by MYB-bHLH transcription factor module
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Nucleus for regulatory factors; downstream enzymes in cytosol/ER/vacuole-associated locations depending on gene
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Fruit-specific expression of snapdragon Del and Ros1 activated broad phenylpropanoid/flavonoid gene expression in tomato fruit. 2. This caused very high anthocyanin accumulation in peel and flesh, intense purple coloration, and a threefold increase in hydrophilic antioxidant capacity. 3. Purple tomato supplementation significantly extended lifespan of Trp53-/- mice compared with red tomato or standard diet.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tomato fruit peel, pericarp, inner flesh, initially near vascular tissue
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant pigmentation and antioxidant defense; in diet, associated with protection against cancer, cardiovascular disease, age-related degeneration, inflammation, obesity/diabetes, and visual decline

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for stacking with anthocyanin regulators because PAL controls entry flux into phenylpropanoid metabolism in Solanaceous species. Boosting PAL in fruit can raise precursor supply for anthocyanins, but should be fruit-specific to avoid whole-plant metabolic burden.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Deaminates phenylalanine to form cinnamic acid, the entry step into phenylpropanoid metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PAL was strongly induced by Del/Ros1, with about 200-fold higher PAL enzyme activity in line N, likely increasing flux to anthocyanins and enabling very high accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Suppression subtractive hybridization; northern blot; enzyme activity assay; microarray support.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Measured in ripening fruit under E8-driven regulator expression; fruit developmental stage relevant.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low/basal in wild-type fruit; strongly induced in Del/Ros1 fruit during ripening.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream; entry/committed gateway to phenylpropanoid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Suppression subtractive hybridization; northern blot; microarray ArrayExpress A-MEXP-1417 / E-MEXP-1830
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors emphasize PAL activity determines flux through phenylpropanoid metabolism in Solanaceous species and contrasts with weaker induction in Lc/C1 tomato studies.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increased phenylpropanoid flux may alter lignin/phenolic balance or divert carbon/nitrogen, though no plant growth defects were reported.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point / flux-determining step in Solanaceous phenylpropanoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Del/Ros1 strongly induced PAL transcript accumulation in tomato fruit. 2. PAL enzymatic activity increased ~200-fold in the strongest line. 3. Elevated precursor flux through phenylpropanoid metabolism likely underpins the exceptionally high anthocyanin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant/pigmentation compounds associated with dietary health benefits

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful engineering target with regulators because CHI may limit flux in tomato skin. Fruit-specific induction or overexpression can improve conversion of chalcones into downstream flavonoids and anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CHI activity was increased threefold transiently during fruit development, contributing to improved flavonoid flux and high anthocyanin/flavonol accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme activity assay; pathway-gene expression analysis by northern blot/microarray; inference from metabolic phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms central intermediate for downstream anthocyanin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient induction during fruit development/ripening under E8-driven Del/Ros1 expression.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Normally limiting in tomato skin; induced transiently in Del/Ros1 fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Northern blot; microarray ArrayExpress A-MEXP-1417 / E-MEXP-1830; enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors note CHI may limit flux through flavonoid metabolism in tomato skin and that Del/Ros1, unlike Lc/C1, induced CHI activity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux enhancement may alter naringenin chalcone and peel flavonol balance; no obvious developmental penalty reported.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a possible limiting step in tomato flavonoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Del/Ros1 increased CHI activity in tomato fruit. 2. CHI induction was transient but may relieve a flavonoid-pathway bottleneck. 3. This likely contributed to high anthocyanin accumulation achieved in purple fruit.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important for establishing anthocyanin flux in tomato fruit; can be stacked with upstream flux genes and regulatory TFs for stronger pigmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts flavanones such as naringenin to dihydroflavonols by 3-hydroxylation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: F3H expression was induced by Del/Ros1, enabling progression from flavanones toward dihydroflavonol intermediates for anthocyanin formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Northern blot verification after not being recovered in subtractive hybridization; pathway induction inferred from metabolite accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor class for downstream anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit ripening context under E8-driven regulator expression.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Undetectable/low in wild-type fruit; induced in Del/Ros1 fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Northern blot; microarray support likely though not individually detailed
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not identified by suppression subtractive hybridization but confirmed as induced by northern blot.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3H expression was induced by Del/Ros1 in tomato fruit. 2. This supplied dihydroflavonol intermediates needed for anthocyanin biosynthesis. 3. Its induction complements broader activation of the flavonoid pathway.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Critical branch-setting enzyme for delphinidin/petunidin-rich anthocyanin engineering in Solanaceous crops. Should be included or activated when DFR substrate specificity favors dihydromyricetin. Valuable for producing purple fruit chemistry with acylated delphinidin-type pigments.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the B-ring of flavonoid intermediates to produce 3',5'-hydroxylated substrates, leading to dihydromyricetin-derived anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Del/Ros1 activated F3'5'H, enabling formation of dihydromyricetin and thereby compatible substrates for tomato DFR, which was essential for anthocyanin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene-expression analysis; mechanistic interpretation based on known DFR substrate specificity and anthocyanin composition (delphinidin/petunidin derivatives).
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor and branch-selecting intermediate for target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit ripening context under E8-driven regulator expression.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Activated in Del/Ros1 fruit; not activated by maize Lc/C1 in prior tomato study.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Subtractive hybridization/northern blot/microarray support for anthocyanin biosynthetic genes; metabolite profiling by HPLC/ESI-MS
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors explicitly identify F3'5'H induction as a key reason Del/Ros1 succeeded where Lc/C1 failed. Delphinidin and petunidin derivatives were the major anthocyanins detected.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Branch redirection may reduce alternative flavonoid products such as kaempferol-derived compounds.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Flavonoid intermediates such as dihydrokaempferol/dihydroquercetin precursors
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Del/Ros1 activated tomato F3'5'H expression. 2. This generated dihydromyricetin-type substrates needed by Solanaceous DFR. 3. F3'5'H induction explains production of delphinidin/petunidin anthocyanins and the success of the engineering strategy.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Purple pigmentation and antioxidant/dietary health-associated function

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Suitable for anthocyanin engineering only when compatible dihydroflavonol substrates are supplied, especially through F3'5'H activation in Solanaceous species. Stack with branch-selection genes rather than editing alone without substrate support.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces dihydroflavonols to leucoanthocyanidins; in Solanaceous species described here as accepting dihydromyricetin but not dihydrokaempferol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DFR participates in converting hydroxylated dihydroflavonols toward anthocyanidins; successful anthocyanin production depended on provision of suitable dihydromyricetin-type substrate via F3'5'H.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene-expression induction plus pathway logic based on known substrate specificity and metabolite outcome.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit ripening context under E8-driven regulator expression.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced as part of the anthocyanin pathway in Del/Ros1 fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Subtractive hybridization/northern blot/microarray support; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes the substrate specificity constraint of Solanaceous DFR as a mechanistic explanation for prior engineering failures.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Substrate specificity may constrain pigment spectrum unless upstream hydroxylation is engineered.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Dihydroflavonols, particularly dihydromyricetin in this system
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; acts as substrate-constrained branch determinant in this system
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DFR was among the induced anthocyanin biosynthetic genes in Del/Ros1 fruit. 2. Tomato DFR substrate specificity requires 3',5'-hydroxylated intermediates for effective anthocyanin production. 3. This dependency helps explain why Del/Ros1 succeeded in inducing anthocyanins.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A necessary late-pathway enzyme for anthocyanin accumulation; can be stacked with regulators and glycosylation/transport genes for strong fruit pigmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ANS expression was induced in Del/Ros1 fruit, supporting formation of anthocyanidin aglycones later converted to stable anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene-expression analysis and metabolite accumulation evidence.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate anthocyanidin precursors of target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced in Del/Ros1 fruit; little/no detectable expression in wild-type fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of the nearly complete late biosynthetic program activated by Del/Ros1.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANS was induced in tomato fruit by Del/Ros1. 2. This enabled conversion of leucoanthocyanidins to anthocyanidins. 3. ANS induction formed part of the coordinated late-pathway activation underlying purple fruit formation.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential stabilization step for engineering fruit anthocyanins; suitable stacking target to increase anthocyanin stability and accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose to anthocyanidins at the 3-O position, forming anthocyanin glucosides.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: 3-GT expression was induced by Del/Ros1, supporting stable anthocyanin formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene-expression analysis; anthocyanin structural identification by HPLC/ESI-MS supports glycosylated products.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Subtractive hybridization/northern blot/microarray support; HPLC/ESI-MS structure data
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Major anthocyanins accumulated as 3,5-diglucosides further decorated with rutinoside/acyl groups.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May channel anthocyanidins away from other conjugates; not reported as detrimental.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Del/Ros1 induced 3-GT expression in tomato fruit. 2. Glycosylation stabilized anthocyanidins into anthocyanins. 3. This modification was consistent with the highly glycosylated anthocyanins identified in purple tomatoes.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for generating rutinosylated anthocyanins typical of tomato and improving pigment stability/solubility; a secondary tailoring target after core pathway activation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Adds rhamnose to flavonoid 3-O-glucosides to form rutinosides/rhamnoglucosides.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: RT was among induced genes supporting formation of rutinosylated anthocyanins detected in purple tomato fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene-expression analysis; anthocyanin structural characterization by MS showing rutinoside-containing anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RT expression increased in Del/Ros1 fruit. 2. Its activity is consistent with the rutinoside-containing anthocyanins accumulated. 3. RT contributes to final anthocyanin tailoring and stability in engineered fruit.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable tailoring step for engineering 3,5-diglucoside anthocyanins with enhanced stability and characteristic purple pigmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Adds glucose at the 5-O position of anthocyanins/flavonoids, generating 3,5-diglucosides.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: 5-GT was induced and is consistent with the major 3,5-diglucoside anthocyanins identified in Del/Ros1 fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene-expression analysis; HPLC/ESI-MS identification of 3,5-diglucosylated anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Major anthocyanins in purple tomato were 3,5-diglucosides acylated with cinnamic acids.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Del/Ros1 induced 5-GT expression in fruit. 2. This supports production of 3,5-diglucosylated anthocyanins. 3. 5-GT contributes to the final stable pigment structures that accumulated at high levels.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to stabilize anthocyanins via acylation, enhancing pigment persistence, color intensity, and potentially bioaccessibility. Pair with glycosylation and vacuolar transport genes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers hydroxycinnamoyl acyl groups onto anthocyanin glycosides, producing acylated anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: A putative anthocyanin acyltransferase gene was induced by Del/Ros1 and likely contributed to accumulation of acylated delphinidin/petunidin 3-rutinoside-5-glucosides.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Suppression subtractive hybridization; northern blot validation; HPLC/ESI-MS structural characterization of acylated anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Suppression subtractive hybridization; northern blot; HPLC/ESI-MS
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Major pigments were acylated with caffeoyl, coumaroyl, or feruloyl groups.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Acylation pattern can alter color hue and possibly bioavailability; not assessed in detail.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Del/Ros1 induced a putative anthocyanin acyltransferase. 2. Purple tomato accumulated heavily acylated anthocyanins. 3. The induced acyltransferase likely stabilizes and diversifies the final anthocyanin products.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A useful auxiliary engineering target to secure high anthocyanin accumulation by promoting vacuolar sequestration and reducing cytosolic instability/toxicity. Best stacked with biosynthetic and modification genes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Functions in conjugation/ligandin-assisted handling associated with anthocyanin transport into the vacuole rather than direct synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: GST was induced in Del/Ros1 fruit and may facilitate transport/sequestration of anthocyanins into the vacuole, supporting high pigment accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Differential expression analysis; pathway model in figure; inference from known anthocyanin transport roles.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Figure text notes GST and PAT may be involved in anthocyanin transport into the vacuole.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered vacuolar transport capacity could affect handling of other phenolics; not reported.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Del/Ros1 induced a GST linked to anthocyanin accumulation. 2. GST likely supports vacuolar sequestration of pigments. 3. Transport capacity probably contributed to stable high anthocyanin storage in tomato fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Vacuoles of tomato fruit peel and flesh cells
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation, antioxidant activity, and safe intracellular storage

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Activated indirectly by Del/Ros1 under fruit-specific E8 transgene system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising support gene for high-pigment engineering because storage transport often limits net accumulation. Stack with biosynthetic regulators and anthocyanin tailoring genes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Mediates or assists anthocyanin transport into the vacuole.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: A putative anthocyanin transporter gene was induced by Del/Ros1 and likely enabled efficient vacuolar accumulation of anthocyanins in purple fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Suppression subtractive hybridization; northern blot validation; inference from pathway model and strong accumulation phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Suppression subtractive hybridization; northern blot; microarray support
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The transporter was previously linked to anthocyanin accumulation in tomato literature cited by the paper.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could affect transport of other vacuolar specialized metabolites; not reported.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Del/Ros1 induced a putative anthocyanin transporter in tomato fruit. 2. Enhanced transport likely permitted stable vacuolar deposition of anthocyanins. 3. Transport competence accompanied the strong biosynthetic activation of purple fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Vacuoles of fruit peel and flesh cells

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: MicroTom; also transferred to Money Maker, Ailsa Craig, VF36
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Del is a high-value engineering regulator for fruit biofortification with anthocyanins when co-expressed with a compatible MYB partner such as Ros1. Deploy with fruit/ripening-specific promoters to confine pigmentation to fruit and minimize pleiotropy. Best used in regulator stacking rather than alone, and in species/backgrounds where target-pathway specificity matches endogenous structural genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: When co-expressed with Ros1, Del strongly activated anthocyanin biosynthesis in tomato fruit, producing intense purple peel and flesh and very high anthocyanin content.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable transgenic co-expression in tomato; northern blot verification; pathway gene induction by subtractive hybridization and microarray; metabolite profiling by HPLC/ESI-MS; antioxidant capacity assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential co-activator of the anthocyanin program; together with Ros1 it functions as a master switch that activates broad structural, modification, and transport genes required for high anthocyanin accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Action visible during fruit ripening because Del was driven by the ripening-associated E8 promoter; pigmentation started late mature green/breaker stage.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgene expression restricted mainly to ripening fruit; no abnormal pigment accumulation in stems or leaves.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In snapdragon, Del functions in anthocyanin induction in flowers.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin biosynthesis, anthocyanin modification, and vacuolar sequestration
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Suppression subtractive hybridization; tomato PROFOOD microarray ArrayExpress A-MEXP-1417 and E-MEXP-1830; HPLC/ESI-MS metabolite profiling
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors attribute success partly to broader target specificity than maize Lc/C1, especially induction of PAL, CHI, and F3'5'H. Del alone was not tested here as sufficient for the full phenotype.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious vegetative defects in transformed plants under fruit-specific expression. Overexpression outside fruit could potentially cause ectopic pigmentation or broader phenylpropanoid perturbation.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: PAL; CHI; F3H; F3'5'H; DFR; ANS; 3-GT; RT; 5-GT; anthocyanin acyltransferase; GST; PAT
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Forms MYB-bHLH regulatory complex with Ros1
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation in combination with Ros1
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates a broad spectrum of anthocyanin biosynthetic, tailoring, and transport genes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Fruit-specific Del expression together with Ros1 induced strong purple pigmentation in tomato peel and flesh. 2. Del/Ros1 activated nearly the full anthocyanin program, including upstream flux, late biosynthesis, modification, and transport genes. 3. This broad target specificity enabled exceptionally high anthocyanin accumulation and increased fruit antioxidant capacity.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tomato fruit peel, pericarp, inner flesh, vacuoles
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation; antioxidant and health-associated dietary compound
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Ripening-dependent E8 promoter activity in tomato fruit

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: MicroTom; also transferred to Money Maker, Ailsa Craig, VF36
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Ros1 is a powerful MYB regulator for anthocyanin biofortification when paired with a compatible bHLH such as Del. Use fruit-specific expression to concentrate anthocyanins in edible tissues. Particularly useful for activating broad pathway specificity in heterologous hosts after target-gene compatibility testing.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: When co-expressed with Del, Ros1 triggered massive anthocyanin accumulation throughout tomato fruit, elevating antioxidant capacity and producing purple tomatoes with dietary efficacy in mice.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable transgenic co-expression in tomato; northern blot verification; structural gene induction by subtractive hybridization and microarray; HPLC/ESI-MS metabolite profiling; antioxidant and feeding assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential MYB partner and major switch for activating anthocyanin accumulation; co-expression with Del is decisive for high-level, whole-fruit pigmentation.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phenotype was ripening-stage dependent because Ros1 expression was driven by the E8 promoter.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgene activity primarily in ripening fruit; pigment spread from vascular tissue to peel, pericarp, and flesh.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In snapdragon, Ros1 functions in flower anthocyanin regulation.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin biosynthesis, anthocyanin modification, vacuolar transport/sequestration
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Suppression subtractive hybridization; PROFOOD microarray ArrayExpress A-MEXP-1417 and E-MEXP-1830; HPLC/ESI-MS
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The combination with Del was chosen because these proteins interact in snapdragon flowers to induce anthocyanin biosynthesis. Broader and more compatible specificity than maize C1/Lc in tomato was crucial.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No visible growth penalty under fruit-specific expression. Constitutive use in other contexts may alter development or phenylpropanoid partitioning.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: PAL; CHI; F3H; F3'5'H; DFR; ANS; 3-GT; RT; 5-GT; anthocyanin acyltransferase; GST; PAT
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Forms MYB-bHLH regulatory complex with Del
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation in combination with Del
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates broad anthocyanin structural and transport gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Ros1, co-expressed with Del in tomato fruit, enabled strong activation of anthocyanin biosynthesis. 2. Ros1/Del induced broad structural and transport gene expression, producing acylated delphinidin/petunidin anthocyanins in peel and flesh. 3. The resulting fruit had increased antioxidant capacity and showed dietary benefits in a mouse cancer model.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tomato fruit peel, pericarp, inner flesh, vacuoles
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation; antioxidant and health-associated dietary compound
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Ripening-dependent E8 promoter activity in tomato fruit

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native/constitutive context not engineered in this study
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ANT1 can promote anthocyanin accumulation in tomato but, based on this paper, it gives only limited skin/subepidermal pigmentation and is less effective than Del/Ros1 for whole-fruit biofortification. It may still be useful in combination with additional partners or pathway genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Prior overexpression of ANT1 caused limited purple coloration on the skin and immediately beneath the skin of tomato fruit.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced prior overexpression study, not directly tested here.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Conditionally effective but not sufficient for high-level whole-fruit anthocyanin enrichment in the context discussed.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as contextual comparator showing that not all anthocyanin regulators are equally effective in tomato fruit.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely TF complex involvement, but not described here
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Transcription factor (anthocyanin regulator; family not specified here)
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes anthocyanin pathway expression but with limited tissue range/effect in tomato fruit
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Prior ANT1 overexpression in tomato gave only limited purple color in fruit skin/subskin. 2. This contrasts with the much stronger whole-fruit effect of Del/Ros1. 3. The comparison highlights the importance of regulator choice and target-gene specificity for metabolic engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tomato fruit skin and subepidermal layers

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified in this paper for comparator study
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Lc alone/with C1 is not optimal for anthocyanin engineering in tomato fruit because target specificity is insufficient; should only be considered with careful pathway compatibility analysis and likely additional engineering of F3'5'H/DFR substrate context.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Prior expression of maize Lc with C1 increased flavonols but failed to induce anthocyanin accumulation in tomato fruit.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced prior transgenic study, not directly tested here.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Comparator regulator showing that not all heterologous anthocyanin TFs are decisive in tomato; ineffective for anthocyanin accumulation under the cited conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors suggest Lc/C1 failed because they did not activate F3'5'H or sufficiently induce PAL/CHI, and tomato DFR substrate specificity then blocked anthocyanin synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin/flavonoid biosynthetic genes; weaker or incomplete activation in tomato
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: MYB-bHLH-type regulatory pairing with C1 implied by prior work
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Induced some flavonoid genes and flavonol accumulation but insufficient anthocyanin-pathway activation in tomato
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Prior expression of maize Lc/C1 in tomato increased flavonols but not anthocyanins. 2. Compared with Del/Ros1, Lc/C1 had poorer target specificity in tomato fruit. 3. Lack of F3'5'H activation and weaker PAL/CHI induction likely explain failure to produce anthocyanins.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tomato fruit (no substantial anthocyanin accumulation reported)

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified in this paper for comparator study
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: C1 with Lc is not an optimal regulator combination for tomato anthocyanin biofortification according to the comparison in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Prior expression of maize C1 with Lc increased flavonols but did not trigger anthocyanin accumulation in tomato fruit.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced prior transgenic study, not directly tested here.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Ineffective comparator for anthocyanin production in tomato fruit under the cited engineering context.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Serves as comparator to underscore species-specific TF target compatibility.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin/flavonoid biosynthetic genes; incomplete activation in tomato
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Pairs with Lc in regulatory complex
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Partial activation insufficient for anthocyanin production in tomato
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Prior Lc/C1 expression increased flavonol levels but not anthocyanins in tomato. 2. Del/Ros1 were more effective because of broader and more compatible target specificity. 3. C1 exemplifies the need to select regulators matched to endogenous pathway architecture.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tomato fruit (no substantial anthocyanin accumulation reported)

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native gene; not transgenically manipulated here
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: DET1 downregulation can increase tomato fruit flavonoids, but as a pleiotropic light-development repressor it is likely less precise than fruit-specific Del/Ros1 for anthocyanin enrichment. Use with caution because broader developmental tradeoffs are plausible.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing DET1 reportedly increased flavonoid levels up to 3.5-fold in tomato fruit in previous work.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced prior silencing study, not directly tested here.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: An indirect negative regulator of flavonoid accumulation, but not the central successful engineering regulator in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Light-mediated developmental repression affecting flavonoid accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as prior modest engineering route compared with the stronger Del/Ros1 approach.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because DET1 represses light-mediated development, its perturbation may cause pleiotropic developmental effects, though not analyzed here.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Not specified here; broad light-responsive/metabolic outputs
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Repression of light-mediated development and associated flavonoid accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Prior DET1 silencing modestly increased tomato flavonoids. 2. This route was less effective than Del/Ros1 for strong anthocyanin enrichment. 3. The comparison highlights the advantage of pathway-specific transcriptional activation over broad developmental derepression.
